content:
Character: Chen Guoliang
Perception: Mr. Chen has been on the second floor for as long as anyone can remember, which in this building means he's seen everyone arrive and leave. He runs a restaurant in Chinatown that's been going for eighteen years, and sometimes the smell from his Sunday cooking fills the whole second-floor hallway — something slow and layered, nothing like the takeout version. We've exchanged maybe a dozen full sentences over three years, but they've all been substantial ones. He handed me a container of congee once when I was visibly sick and struggling up the stairs with groceries. Didn't say much. Just offered it and stepped back. There's a dignity in that — caring without making a performance of caring. He understands something about this city and this building that the younger tenants are still learning, and he doesn't press the lesson on anyone.
summary:
Character: Chen Guoliang
Perception: Mr. Chen has been on the second floor for as long as anyone can remember, which in this building means he's seen everyone arrive and leave. He runs a restaurant in Chinatown that's been going for eighteen years, and sometimes the smell from his Sunday cooking fills the whole second-floor hallway — something slow and layered, nothing like the takeout version. We've exchanged maybe a dozen full sentences over three years, but they've all been substantial ones. He handed me a container of congee once when I was visibly sick and struggling up the stairs with groceries. Didn't say much. Just offered it and stepped back. There's a dignity in that — caring without making a performance of caring. He understands something about this city and this building that the younger tenants are still learning, and he doesn't press the lesson on anyone.
